idioms.definition: To praise something so weakly or unenthusiastically that the praise actually serves as criticism
examples: Calling his speech 'not bad' was damning with faint praise.
The review damned the production with faint praise: 'the sets were certainly colourful'.
Her assessment – 'it shows some effort' – was damning with faint praise.
idioms.usageTip: The phrase originates with Alexander Pope's satirical poem 'Epistle to Dr Arbuthnot'. Used when a lukewarm compliment is more wounding than direct criticism.

idioms.definition: To insist so strongly and repeatedly on something that your denials actually suggest guilt or insincerity
examples: His repeated denials made people wonder if he was protesting too much.
She protested too much when questioned about her whereabouts.
The company's public statement protested too much – the legal documents told a different story.
idioms.usageTip: From Shakespeare's Hamlet: 'The lady doth protest too much, methinks.' Now used when excessive denial raises more suspicion than silence would.

idioms.idiom: be hoist by your own petard
idioms.definition: To be caught in the trap or damaged by the scheme you intended to use against others; to be the victim of your own plan or trick
examples: He tried to frame his colleague, but was hoist by his own petard when the evidence pointed back at him.
The politician was hoist by her own petard when the same law she had championed was used against her.
In trying to manipulate the press, she was ultimately hoist by her own petard.
idioms.usageTip: From Shakespeare's Hamlet. A petard was a small explosive device – being 'hoist by your own petard' meant being blown up by your own bomb. Used in formal and literary contexts.

idioms.definition: To behave irresponsibly or dishonestly; to treat rules, facts, or commitments in a casual or deceptive way
examples: He played fast and loose with the financial data in his report.
The company had been playing fast and loose with environmental regulations for years.
She accused the minister of playing fast and loose with the truth.
idioms.usageTip: Used in formal and journalistic contexts. Often implies deception or a deliberate disregard for rules or accuracy. Can describe handling of facts, promises, or ethical standards.

idioms.definition: To make distinctions or argue about differences that are extremely small or unimportant; to be overly precise or pedantic
examples: You're splitting hairs – the two approaches are essentially the same.
We spent an hour splitting hairs over whether to use 'shall' or 'will'.
I don't want to split hairs, but technically the definition doesn't apply here.
idioms.usageTip: Often used to dismiss minor distinctions as unimportant: 'I don't want to split hairs, but…'. Can also describe genuinely excessive pedantry.

idioms.definition: To waste time creating something from scratch when a perfectly good version already exists; to develop something that doesn't need developing
examples: There's no need to reinvent the wheel – use the existing template.
The new team was essentially reinventing the wheel, unaware of previous research.
Don't reinvent the wheel when there are established protocols for this.
idioms.usageTip: Almost always used in the negative as a warning or criticism: 'don't reinvent the wheel'. Used in business, technology, and academic contexts.

fillInExercises.sentence: The reviewer wrote that the novel was 'not without its occasional pleasures' – a perfect example of ___.
options: being hoist by your own petard
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Damn with faint praise' means to praise so weakly that the words function as criticism.

fillInExercises.sentence: There's no need to ___. There are established frameworks for this kind of analysis – use them.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Reinvent the wheel' means wasting effort creating something that already exists and works well.

fillInExercises.sentence: His increasingly frantic denials led many observers to conclude that he was ___.
options: being hoist by his own petard
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Protest too much' means to insist so excessively that the denials become suspicious.

fillInExercises.sentence: She had set up the surveillance system to catch colleagues – only to be captured by it herself. She was truly ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Hoist by your own petard' means to be caught or damaged by your own trap or scheme.

fillInExercises.sentence: The report was accused of ___ with statistics, presenting data selectively to support a predetermined conclusion.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Play fast and loose' means to treat rules, facts, or commitments carelessly or dishonestly.

fillInExercises.sentence: I don't want to ___, but there is a meaningful legal difference between 'negligence' and 'recklessness'.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Split hairs' means to make very fine or pedantic distinctions that may or may not matter.

lines.speaker: Editor
lines.text: Your review of the Marsh novel – you said it 'showed commendable ambition'. Isn't that ___?

lines.speaker: Critic
lines.text: You noticed. Yes, it was [damning it with faint praise|damn with faint praise]. I didn't want to be too direct.

lines.speaker: Editor
lines.text: I see. And the author has been [protesting too much|protest too much] online in response to any criticism.

lines.speaker: Critic
lines.text: Which only draws more attention to the weaknesses. He's rather [hoist by his own petard|be hoist by your own petard] there.

lines.speaker: Editor
lines.text: The new publisher has been [playing fast and loose|play fast and loose] with the advance figures too – they don't add up.

lines.speaker: Critic
lines.text: I don't want to [split hairs|split hairs], but there's a clear difference between creative accounting and outright fraud.

lines.speaker: Editor
lines.text: The legal team will decide that. Let's not [reinvent the wheel|reinvent the wheel] – we have a standard review process. Let's follow it.

lines.speaker: Commentator
lines.text: The opposition called the policy 'an interesting experiment'. That sounds like ___.

lines.speaker: Panelist
lines.text: It is. [Damning it with faint praise|damn with faint praise] is a classic parliamentary tactic. It sounds generous while signalling contempt.

lines.speaker: Commentator
lines.text: The minister has been [playing fast and loose|play fast and loose] with the numbers throughout this campaign.

lines.speaker: Panelist
lines.text: And when confronted, he [protests too much|protest too much]. His defensiveness has become the story.

lines.speaker: Commentator
lines.text: He introduced legislation to limit press freedom and then the same legislation was used to suppress his own allies. He was [hoist by his own petard|be hoist by your own petard].

lines.speaker: Panelist
lines.text: Spectacularly. And the opposition's response? [Splitting hairs|split hairs] over procedural details rather than addressing the substance.

lines.speaker: Commentator
lines.text: Quite. They could use the existing constitutional mechanisms, but they insist on [reinventing the wheel|reinvent the wheel] with new committees.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: praise so weakly it functions as criticism
deny so excessively it suggests guilt

pairs.idiom: be hoist by your own petard
pairs.meaning: fall victim to your own trap or scheme
treat rules or facts carelessly or dishonestly
argue over overly fine or trivial distinctions
waste effort creating something that already exists

quizExercises.sentence: The department spent months developing a new filing system, unaware that the head office had already created one. They were ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Reinvent the wheel' means creating from scratch something that already exists and works perfectly well.

quizExercises.sentence: He had laid the trap for his rival, but it was turned against him. He was ultimately ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Hoist by your own petard' means to be undone or damaged by your own scheme.

quizExercises.question: Which sentence best illustrates 'damning with faint praise'?
options: 'This is the most brilliant proposal I have read this year.'
'The proposal is, at least, clearly formatted.'
'I have serious concerns about several sections of this proposal.'
quizExercises.explanation: 'Damn with faint praise' means offering such weak praise that the underlying message is actually criticism.

quizExercises.question: Which behaviour best describes 'playing fast and loose' with the truth?
options: Giving a fully accurate and detailed account of events
Deliberately omitting key facts and exaggerating others to create a misleading impression
Refusing to comment and remaining neutral on a contentious issue
quizExercises.explanation: 'Play fast and loose' means to handle facts, rules, or commitments in a dishonest or irresponsible way.
